title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: : Type 1 Credit Prices Fall; Trade Volumes Continue to Decline WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights: (18th â€“ 24th August 2025)
  • A total of 99,547 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 30.78% fall week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 45.61% fall WoW as 182,647 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $56.47/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 1.10% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers increased by 1.93% in price, reaching $57.44/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 1.60% WoW; on 26th August, the spot price stood at $56.50/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $57.88/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices declined by 0.92% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 prices fell by 2.75%, 2.78% and 2.75% WoW respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the last week, Type 1 trade prices fell by 1.10%; trade volumes declined. In this week, U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) announced its action on the backlog of 175 Small Refinery Exemption (SRE) petitions from 38 small refineries for 2016 â€“ 2024 compliance years. In consultation with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE), EPA critically reviewed and considered information submitted by each petitioning small refinery. It also evaluated each SRE petition consistent with the Clean Air Act and case law. After carefully reviewing all information, EPA has granted full exemptions to 63 petitions, granting partial exemptions to 77 petitions, denying 28 petitions, and determining 7 petitions to be ineligible. Moreover, EPA has disapproved of Californiaâ€™s Heavy-Duty (HD) Inspection and Maintenance (I/M) Requirements as it applies to out-of-state and out-of-country vehicles. Thereby, it has proposed to deny these actions due to concerns that this requirement violates the U.S. Constitutionâ€™s Commerce Clause and this inconsistency with Federal law also violates Section 110 of the Clean Air Act. California state agencies such as the California Air Resources Board (CARB), California Energy Commission (CEC), Governorâ€™s Office of Business and Economic Development (GO-Biz), California State Transportation Agency (CalSTA), and Department of Consumer Affairs (DCA) delivered a report to Gov. Gavin Newsom highlighting six priority actions to accelerate ZEV adoption, protecting the Low Carbon Fuel Standard, bolstering incentives for low-income drivers, expanding reliable charging and hydrogen fueling, lowering charging costs, strengthening consumer protections and regulations, and sustaining Californiaâ€™s leadership despite federal rollbacks.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_602765" align="alignnone" width="792"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.10% WoW decline, falling from $57.10 to $56.47 this week, and all non-zero credit prices increased by 1.93% WoW, from $56.35 to $57.44. Type 1 trades witnessed 99,547 credits exchanged, reflecting a 30.78% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a 45.61% fall WoW, reaching 182,647 credits  

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_602767" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 1.60% WoW, reaching $56.63/MT. On 26th August, the spot price was $56.50/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $57.88/MT as of 26th August.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_602768" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 28 trades were reported in the market, with no high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $69.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 14,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $43.50 per credit of one Type 1 transaction reported for 5,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 15,000 credits for Type 1 at $57.75, while the smallest volume of transfer involved 2,047 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $57.25.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_602769" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $56.64 in August so far, up from $47.99 in July. Trade volumes for the month of August stood at 1.47 M as of 26th August, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_602770" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 18th â€“ 24th August, total VPT recorded an increase of 0.46% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.

Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices declined by 0.92% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 prices fell by 2.75%, 2.78% and 2.75% WoW respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • SolAmerica Energy Finalizes $100 million Revolving Credit Facility with Deutsche Bank to Fund Portfolio of Solar Generation Assets: SolAmerica Energy (SAE), an Atlanta-based developer of distributed solar projects owned by AB CarVal funds, has closed a $100 million revolving credit facility with Deutsche Bank to finance project development, equipment purchases, and construction across the U.S. Southeast, East Coast, and Midwest. The deal, Deutsche Bank acting as Administrative and Collateral Agent, marks SAEâ€™s third financing since its 2023 acquisition by AB CarVal, following a $50 million construction loan with KeyBank and an $80 million+ tax equity transaction with Key Equipment Finance. Executives from SAE, Deutsche Bank, and AB CarVal highlighted the facility as a sign of strong lender confidence and a step toward scaling affordable, reliable renewable energy to meet rising U.S. demand.
  • CARB Reports to the Governor in Response to Executive Order on ZEV Deployment: California agencies delivered a report to Gov. Gavin Newsom outlining six priority actions to accelerate ZEV adoption, protecting the Low Carbon Fuel Standard, bolstering incentives for low-income drivers, expanding reliable charging and hydrogen fueling, lowering charging costs, strengthening consumer protections and regulations, and adopting ZEV-first fleet procurement. A key proposal is a statewide indirect source rule to curb freight-related emissions in overburdened communities. Developed with input from 1,000+ participants and 200 comments, the recommendations aim to expand clean vehicle adoption, safeguard consumers, and sustain Californiaâ€™s leadership despite federal rollbacks.
  • EIA: US Ethanol Output: Ethanol production in the United States averaged 1.093 million barrels per day (bpd), up 12,000 bpd week-on-week and 21,000 bpd, or 2.4%, higher than in the same week last year, according to data released by the Energy Information Administration (EIA). Four-week average output at 1.087 million bpd was 1,000 bpd above the same four weeks last year.Midwest ethanol production averaged 1.034 million bpd, up 13,000 bpd week-on-week and 20,000 bpd, or 1.9%, higher than in the same week last year. Four-week average output at 1.03 million bpd was 5,000 bpd above the same four weeks last year.
  • EPA Announces Action on Small Refinery Exemptions, Continues Work to Get Renewable Fuel Standard Program Back on Track: S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is acting on the backlog of 175 Small Refinery Exemption (SRE) petitions from 38 small refineries for 2016 â€“ 2024 compliance years. In consultation with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE), EPA reviewed and considered information submitted by each petitioning small refinery. EPA then evaluated each SRE petition consistent with the Clean Air Act and case law. After carefully reviewing all information, EPA is granting full exemptions to 63 petitions, granting partial exemptions to 77 petitions, denying 28 petitions, and determining 7 petitions to be ineligible.
  • Nearly $8 Million Is Now Available To Support Innovation in Low-Carbon Fuels In New York: The New York State Energy Research and Development Authority (NYSERDA) today announced nearly $8 million is now available to support innovation in the development of low-carbon fuels in New York State. The competitive Waste to Low-Carbon Fuels Pilot and Demonstration solicitation supports innovation projects that pilot and scale converting waste feedstock from organic and inorganic discarded materials to energy in hard-to-electrify sectors of the economy.
  • Governor Hochul Announces More Than $11 Million Awarded to Clean Hydrogen Research and Development Projects: Governor Kathy Hochul announced more than $11 million has been awarded to five clean hydrogen research and development projects. The awarded projects will demonstrate new technology designs, cost reductions associated with clean hydrogen storage and distribution, evaluate large-scale clean hydrogen storage opportunities, and deploy zero-emission hydrogen-powered transportation. The announcement helps to address key barriers to the wider adoption of clean hydrogen as a potential solution to decarbonize industrial processes, hard-to-electrify sectors, such as transportation, and for grid support in the Stateâ€™s energy transition.
  • Hydrogen Infrastructure: Port of South Louisiana Tenders First E-Methanol Fueling Barge: Port of South Louisiana is in the works: a cutting-edge hydrogen fueling barge ready to pump e-methanol and other green fuels straight into ships, totally changing the game for marine refueling.The port has put out a call for bids on a custom-built vessel designed to carry Grade C flammable liquids. At its heart is e-methanolâ€”a synthetic marine fuel crafted by combining green hydrogen from renewable electrolysis with captured COâ‚‚. In plain terms, this barge turns the river into a refueling superhighway. Itâ€™s powered by a Build Back Better Regional Challenge grant from the U.S. Economic Development Administration.
  • EPA Takes Action to Deny Californiaâ€™s Latest Illegal Power Grab on Heavy-Duty Vehicles : S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is proposing to disapprove of Californiaâ€™s Heavy-Duty (HD) Inspection and Maintenance (I/M) Requirements as it applies to out-of-state and out-of-country vehicles. The HD I/M requirement in the State Implementation Plan (SIP) submitted by California establishes an I/M program for any heavy-duty vehicle driven in the stateâ€”regardless of registration. EPA is proposing to deny these actions due to concerns that this requirement violates the U.S. Constitutionâ€™s Commerce Clause and this inconsistency with Federal law also violates Section 110 of the Clean Air Act.
